result.markdown:
--- title: "Functional programming for ecological data" date: 2026-07-21 categories: [functional programming, purrr, data.table, dplyr] description: "Twenty ~10-minute quests — use understanding to verify AI-generated R, from base functions to purrr, furrr, and data.table." toc: true toc-depth: 2 format: html: code-fold: false --- ## Use AI to defeat AI Large language models are excellent at **pattern-matching** R code they have seen before. They are weaker at knowing *your* survey design, which column is the key, or whether `map()` should have been `map2()`. If you only copy-paste, the bug ships with the manuscript. This track trains the skill AI cannot give you: **reading and owning the code**. Each quest is ~10 minutes — short segments improve retention in technical material ([Mayer, 2009](https://doi.org/10.1016/B978-0-12-374921-5.00009-3)). We use a predictable rhythm from classroom research ([Rosenshine, 2012](https://doi.org/10.1177/0031721712452808)): 1. **Tell you** what you will accomplish and which packages you need. 2. **Explain** any new function *before* it appears in a chunk. 3. **Show** annotated code — one comment per line so you can narrate it aloud. 4. **Apply** where this shows up in marine ecology workflows (and what to ask AI to do next). When AI suggests a pipeline, you will know enough to say *“show me the list structure first”* or *“that needs `safely()` for my QC flags.”* That is the win. **Season 1:** 20 quests · same reprex dataset throughout · mark quests done in your script header for a cheap **retrieval** boost ([Roediger & Butler, 2011](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jml.2010.09.004)). ::: {.quest-hub}

Season 1 · Functional programming

You vs the autocomplete: build recipes you trust, then let AI draft the boring lines. Roadmap for quests 21–100 at the bottom.

::: {.quest-levels} ::: {.quest-level} **Level 1** Scout · base R · quests 1–5 ::: ::: {.quest-level} **Level 2** Wrangler · dplyr · quests 6–10 ::: ::: {.quest-level} **Level 3** Mapper · purrr · quests 11–16 ::: ::: {.quest-level} **Level 4** Parallel diver · furrr & data.table · quests 17–20 ::: ::: ::: ## Reprex: reef transect counts Before Quest 1, create an RStudio project and run this once. **`tibble()`** builds a modern data frame; **`set.seed()`** fixes randomness so your reprex matches ours when you ask AI for help. ::: {.quest-learn} ::: {.quest-learn__row} **Packages** `base R`, **`tibble`** (install via `install.packages("tibble")` if needed — it ships with the tidyverse). ::: ::: {.quest-learn__row} **By the end you will** Have a 60-row table of simulated belt-transect counts you can paste into any forum or prompt. ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} set.seed(427) # fix randomness so counts match this tutorial exactly reef_transects <- tibble::tibble( # build a tidy data frame transect_id = rep(sprintf("T%02d", 1:12), each = 5), # 12 transects, 5 species rows each site = rep(rep(c("North", "South", "East"), length.out = 12), each = 5), # reef site label species = rep( # five fish names repeated across transects c("Parrotfish", "Surgeonfish", "Grouper", "Snapper", "Damselfish"), times = 12 ), count = rpois(60, lambda = c(8, 12, 3, 5, 20)), # Poisson counts — common for count data depth_m = rep(round(runif(12, 2, 15), 1), each = 5) # depth per transect, repeated per species row ) reef_transects # print to confirm structure ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 60 × 5 transect_id site species count depth_m 1 T01 North Parrotfish 4 6.2 2 T01 North Surgeonfish 12 6.2 3 T01 North Grouper 1 6.2 4 T01 North Snapper 7 6.2 5 T01 North Damselfish 21 6.2 6 T02 South Parrotfish 6 5.2 7 T02 South Surgeonfish 7 5.2 8 T02 South Grouper 2 5.2 9 T02 South Snapper 9 5.2 10 T02 South Damselfish 20 5.2 # ℹ 50 more rows ``` ::: ::: ::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"} ## RStudio habit Save as `00-reprex-reef.R`. Run line-by-line (`Cmd/Ctrl + Enter`). When AI gives you code, paste into a **blank script** first — never straight into your only copy of the analysis. ::: --- ::: {.quest-step} ::: {.quest-step__meta} Quest 1 · ~10 min · Level 1 — Scout ::: ### What is a function, really? {.unnumbered} +50 XP ::: {.quest-learn} ::: {.quest-learn__row} **Packages** `base R` only. ::: ::: {.quest-learn__row} **By the end you will** Define a small function, call it with named arguments, and inspect a built-in function object — so AI-generated `function(...)` blocks are not magic. ::: ::: {.quest-learn__row} **Apply it later** Wrap any ecology calculation you repeat (CPUE, biomass, richness) in a named function, then ask AI to *extend* your function instead of inventing a new one from scratch. ::: ::: **What you'll learn:** A function is a **named recipe**. **`function(arg1, arg2) { ... }`** creates it; **`mean`**, **`sum`**, **`lm`** are functions you already call. Printing **`mean`** shows **formals** (arguments), **body** (code), and **environment** — the three parts every function has ([Wickham, 2014](https://adv-r.hadley.nz/function-objects.html)). ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} mean # show the function object (formals, body, environment) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` function (x, ...) UseMethod("mean") ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} fish_biomass <- function(count, mean_weight_kg) { # define inputs: counts and average weight count * mean_weight_kg # body: multiply vector count by scalar or vector weight } # implicit return of last evaluated line fish_biomass( # call our recipe on one row of data count = reef_transects$count[1], # first count in the table mean_weight_kg = 0.4 # example weight in kg for that species group ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 1.6 ``` ::: ::: **Check yourself:** Write **`richness()`** that takes a data frame and returns **`length(unique(species_column))`**. Test on **`filter(reef_transects, site == "North")`**. If AI writes it for you, read each line aloud before running. ::: ::: {.quest-step} ::: {.quest-step__meta} Quest 2 · ~10 min ::: ### Vectorised thinking vs row-wise loops {.unnumbered} +50 XP ::: {.quest-learn} ::: {.quest-learn__row} **Packages** **`dplyr`**, **`ggplot2`**, **`plotly`** — `install.packages(c("dplyr", "ggplot2", "plotly"))` once. ::: ::: {.quest-learn__row} **By the end you will** Add a transformed column with **`mutate()`** and **`log1p()`** (log of 1 + x, safe for zeros) without a loop, and plot depth vs log-count. ::: ::: {.quest-learn__row} **Apply it later** Any time AI suggests `for (i in 1:nrow(df))`, ask whether **`mutate()`** across a column is enough — often it is for deterministic transforms on tidy data. ::: ::: **What you'll learn:** **`mutate()`** adds or changes columns on a data frame. The pipe **`|>`** passes the left side as the first argument to the right side. **`log1p(count)`** is vectorised: R applies it to **every row at once**. **`ggplot()`** + **`aes()`** map variables to axes; **`geom_point()`** draws points. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} reef_transects <- reef_transects |> # start with our reprex table mutate(log_count = log1p(count)) # new column: log(1 + count), handles zero counts ggplot(reef_transects, aes(x = depth_m, y = log_count, color = site)) + # map depth, log count, colour by site geom_point(size = 2, alpha = 0.85) + # draw one point per row theme_minimal(base_size = 13) + # clean theme for slides and web labs( # axis and title labels title = "Log counts vs depth", subtitle = "Vectorised mutate — no explicit loop", y = "log1p(count)" ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-html/quest-2-1.png){width=840} ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.quest-step} ::: {.quest-step__meta} Quest 3 · ~10 min ::: ### `apply()` — functions on matrix margins {.unnumbered} +50 XP ::: {.quest-learn} ::: {.quest-learn__row} **Packages** **`dplyr`**, **`tidyr`**, **`tibble`**. ::: ::: {.quest-learn__row} **By the end you will** Turn a long species table into a matrix and use **`apply(X, MARGIN, FUN)`** to sum rows (transects) or average columns (species). ::: ::: {.quest-learn__row} **Apply it later** Acoustic telemetry matrices, presence–absence grids, and monthly catch tables often start as matrices — **`apply()`** is still what many AI scripts use; you should recognise **`MARGIN = 1`** (rows) vs **`2`** (columns). ::: ::: **What you'll learn:** **`select()`** keeps columns. **`pivot_wider()`** spreads species counts into columns. **`column_to_rownames()`** moves transect IDs to row names. **`as.matrix()`** makes a numeric matrix. **`apply()`** applies **`sum`** or **`mean`** along margin **`1`** (each row) or **`2`** (each column). ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} count_matrix <- reef_transects |> # long-format counts select(transect_id, species, count) |> # keep keys and response pivot_wider(names_from = species, values_from = count) |> # one column per species column_to_rownames("transect_id") |> # transect ID becomes row name as.matrix() # apply() wants a matrix transect_totals <- apply(count_matrix, 1, sum) # margin 1: sum each row (one total per transect) head(transect_totals) # first six transect totals ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` T01 T02 T03 T04 T05 T06 45 44 54 34 51 63 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} species_means <- apply(count_matrix, 2, mean) # margin 2: mean each column (one mean per species) round(species_means, 2) # round for readable output ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Parrotfish Surgeonfish Grouper Snapper Damselfish 7.33 10.58 3.75 4.92 22.67 ``` ::: ::: **Check yourself:** Which margin answers *“how many fish per transect?”* Which answers *“average count per species across transects?”* ::: ::: {.quest-step} ::: {.quest-step__meta} Quest 4 · ~10 min ::: ### `split()` and `lapply()` — lists by group {.unnumbered} +50 XP ::: {.quest-learn} ::: {.quest-learn__row} **Packages** `base R` only. ::: ::: {.quest-learn__row} **By the end you will** Split counts by site into a list and summarise each element with **`lapply()`**, always getting a **list** back (same length as input). ::: ::: {.quest-learn__row} **Apply it later** Per-receiver detection lists, per-animal ID tracks, or per-trip dive logs — ecology is full of “do this for each group.” **`split()` + `lapply()`** is the pattern **`purrr`** improves in Quest 11. ::: ::: **What you'll learn:** **`split(x, f)`** breaks vector **`x`** into a list by factor **`f`**. **`lapply(list, FUN)`** runs **`FUN`** on each list element. Anonymous functions look like **`function(x) { ... }`** when you have not named the recipe yet. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} by_site <- split(reef_transects$count, reef_transects$site) # list of count vectors, one per site site_summaries <- lapply(by_site, function(x) { # for each site's counts... c(mean = mean(x), sd = sd(x), n = length(x)) # return mean, sd, and sample size }) # result is a list with names North, South, East site_summaries # inspect each site's summary vector ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` $East mean sd n 10.950000 7.667258 20.000000 $North mean sd n 8.800000 7.613008 20.000000 $South mean sd n 9.800000 7.324077 20.000000 ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} site_mean_df <- tibble::tibble( # small table for plotting site = names(site_summaries), # site names from the list mean_count = vapply(site_summaries, function(x) x["mean"], numeric(1)) # extract mean from each list element ) ggplot(site_mean_df, aes(x = reorder(site, mean_count), y = mean_count, fill = site)) + # order bars by height geom_col(show.legend = FALSE, width = 0.65) + # bar chart scale_fill_manual(values = c(North = "#1f5460", South = "#2a9d8f", East = "#0077b6")) + # reef palette theme_minimal(base_size = 13) + labs(x = NULL, y = "Mean count", title = "Mean fish count by site") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-html/quest-4-viz-1.png){width=840} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.quest-step} ::: {.quest-step__meta} Quest 5 · ~10 min ::: ### `sapply()` and the “simplify” trap {.unnumbered} +50 XP ::: {.quest-learn} ::: {.quest-learn__row} **Packages** `base R` only (uses **`by_site`** from Quest 4). ::: ::: {.quest-learn__row} **By the end you will** Use **`sapply()`** when every result has the same type and length, and know when AI should **not** use it (unequal lengths → stick with **`lapply()`** or purrr). ::: ::: {.quest-learn__row} **Apply it later** When generated code uses **`sapply()`** on heterogeneous model outputs, replace with **`map()`** / **`map_dbl()`** (Quest 12) so type surprises do not break **`bind_rows()`**. ::: ::: **What you'll learn:** **`sapply()`** is **`lapply()`** plus automatic simplification to a vector or matrix. That is convenient for **`mean`** per site, dangerous when each group returns a different length. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} sapply(by_site, mean) # each site returns one number → simplified to named vector ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` East North South 10.95 8.80 9.80 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # If summaries had different lengths, sapply() could return an unpredictable shape. # AI often hides that bug — prefer lapply() or purrr until Quest 11. ``` ::: ::: --- ::: {.quest-step} ::: {.quest-step__meta} Quest 6 · ~10 min · Level 2 — Wrangler ::: ### `across()` — one `summarise()`, many columns {.unnumbered} +50 XP ::: {.quest-learn} ::: {.quest-learn__row} **Packages** **`dplyr`**. ::: ::: {.quest-learn__row} **By the end you will** Compute mean and sd for several numeric columns per **`site`** in one **`summarise()`**, using **`across()`** instead of copy-pasted **`mean(x)`** lines. ::: ::: {.quest-learn__row} **Apply it later** Reef monitoring with depth, temperature, and count — **`across(where(is.numeric), ...)`** is the tidy pattern AI should use instead of twelve **`mutate()`** lines. ::: ::: **What you'll learn:** **`group_by()`** defines groups. **`summarise()`** collapses each group to one row. **`across(cols, .fns)`** applies functions to multiple columns. **`all_of()`** selects columns by name safely. **`.names = "{.col}_{.fn}"`** controls output column names ([Wickham et al., 2023](https://doi.org/10.32614/RJ-2023-023)). ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} numeric_cols <- c("count", "depth_m") # columns we want summaries for reef_transects |> group_by(site) |> # one row per site in the output summarise( across( # apply both mean and sd to each numeric column all_of(numeric_cols), # only count and depth_m list(mean = mean, sd = sd), # named list of functions .names = "{.col}_{.fn}" # e.g. count_mean, depth_m_sd ), .groups = "drop" # ungroup after summarise ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 3 × 5 site count_mean count_sd depth_m_mean depth_m_sd 1 East 11.0 7.67 5.92 2.60 2 North 8.8 7.61 7.58 0.869 3 South 9.8 7.32 9.28 2.56 ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.quest-step} ::: {.quest-step__meta} Quest 7 · ~10 min ::: ### Custom functions inside `summarise()` and `across()` {.unnumbered} +50 XP ::: {.quest-learn} ::: {.quest-learn__row} **Packages** **`dplyr`**. ::: ::: {.quest-learn__row} **By the end you will** Write **`cv()`** (coefficient of variation) and use it inside **`summarise()`** to compare count variability by species. ::: ::: {.quest-learn__row} **Apply it later** Index of aggregation, CV of CPUE, or detection probability variance — put the ecology in a named function, then let **`across()`** apply it everywhere. ::: ::: **What you'll learn:** **`cv()`** is **`sd(x) / mean(x)`**. **`arrange(desc(...))`** sorts descending. **`summarise(cv_count = cv(count))`** passes the **`count`** column into **`cv`**. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} cv <- function(x) { # coefficient of variation: unitless spread sd(x) / mean(x) # sd divided by mean } reef_transects |> group_by(species) |> # one row per species summarise(cv_count = cv(count), .groups = "drop") |> # apply cv to counts in each group arrange(desc(cv_count)) # most variable species first ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 5 × 2 species cv_count 1 Grouper 0.711 2 Parrotfish 0.528 3 Snapper 0.463 4 Surgeonfish 0.256 5 Damselfish 0.140 ``` ::: ::: **Try:** Group by **`site`**, use **`across(where(is.numeric), cv)`** inside **`summarise()`**. ::: ::: {.quest-step} ::: {.quest-step__meta} Quest 8 · ~10 min ::: ### `group_modify()` — custom data frame per group {.unnumbered} +50 XP ::: {.quest-learn} ::: {.quest-learn__row} **Packages** **`dplyr`**. ::: ::: {.quest-learn__row} **By the end you will** Return the **top two species by total count** within each site using **`group_modify()`**, which passes each group's data as **`.x`**. ::: ::: {.quest-learn__row} **Apply it later** “Top receivers per shark,” “best months per tag” — whenever each group needs a **small table** back, not one number, **`group_modify()`** beats a fragile loop. ::: ::: **What you'll learn:** **`group_modify(.x, ~ { ... })`** runs on each group; **`.x`** is that group's rows. **`slice_max(total, n = 2)`** keeps the two largest **`total`** values ([dplyr slice docs](https://dplyr.tidyverse.org/reference/slice.html)). ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} top_species_per_site <- reef_transects |> group_by(site) |> # split by reef site group_modify(~ { # .x is all rows for one site .x |> group_by(species) |> # species within this site summarise(total = sum(count), .groups = "drop") |> # total count per species slice_max(total, n = 2) # keep top two species by abundance }) top_species_per_site ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 6 × 3 # Groups: site [3] site species total 1 East Damselfish 96 2 East Surgeonfish 46 3 North Damselfish 88 4 North Surgeonfish 41 5 South Damselfish 88 6 South Surgeonfish 40 ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.quest-step} ::: {.quest-step__meta} Quest 9 · ~10 min ::: ### `.by` — per-group columns without `group_by()` {.unnumbered} +50 XP ::: {.quest-learn} ::: {.quest-learn__row} **Packages** **`dplyr`** ≥ 1.1.0. ::: ::: {.quest-learn__row} **By the end you will** Add **`mean_count_site`** within each **`site`** while keeping all rows, using **`.by = site`** inside **`mutate()`**. ::: ::: {.quest-learn__row} **Apply it later** Compare each transect to the site mean, or each detection to the array mean — **`.by`** keeps long tables long (good before joins or maps). ::: ::: **What you'll learn:** **`.by = site`** tells **`mutate()`** to compute **`mean(count)`** separately within each site, then broadcast the result to every row in that site. **`select()`** picks columns; **`head()`** shows the first rows. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} reef_transects |> mutate( # add columns without collapsing rows mean_count_site = mean(count), # mean count within each site .by = site # grouping column for the calculation above ) |> select(site, species, count, mean_count_site) |> # keep readable columns head(6) # preview ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 6 × 4 site species count mean_count_site 1 North Parrotfish 4 8.8 2 North Surgeonfish 12 8.8 3 North Grouper 1 8.8 4 North Snapper 7 8.8 5 North Damselfish 21 8.8 6 South Parrotfish 6 9.8 ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.quest-step} ::: {.quest-step__meta} Quest 10 · ~10 min ::: ### Refactor repeated logic into a function + `group_modify()` {.unnumbered} +50 XP ::: {.quest-learn} ::: {.quest-learn__row} **Packages** **`dplyr`**. ::: ::: {.quest-learn__row} **By the end you will** Encapsulate **`summarise_abundance()`** and apply it to every **`site × species`** combination — the pattern behind many AI-generated “split-apply-combine” scripts. ::: ::: {.quest-learn__row} **Apply it later** Give AI **your** function definition in the prompt: *“Use `summarise_abundance()` on each tag ID.”* You stay in control of definitions; AI fills the boilerplate. ::: ::: **What you'll learn:** **`n_distinct(transect_id)`** counts unique transects. **`group_by(site, species)`** creates cross-classified groups. **`group_modify(~ summarise_abundance(.x))`** applies your function to each group's data frame **`.x`**. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} summarise_abundance <- function(df) { # df is one group's rows df |> summarise( # collapse this group to one row total = sum(count), # total fish counted n_transects = n_distinct(transect_id), # how many transects contributed .groups = "drop" ) } reef_transects |> group_by(site, species) |> # every site–species combination group_modify(~ summarise_abundance(.x)) # run our recipe on each piece ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 15 × 4 # Groups: site, species [15] site species total n_transects 1 East Damselfish 96 4 2 East Grouper 24 4 3 East Parrotfish 35 4 4 East Snapper 18 4 5 East Surgeonfish 46 4 6 North Damselfish 88 4 7 North Grouper 8 4 8 North Parrotfish 21 4 9 North Snapper 18 4 10 North Surgeonfish 41 4 11 South Damselfish 88 4 12 South Grouper 13 4 13 South Parrotfish 32 4 14 South Snapper 23 4 15 South Surgeonfish 40 4 ``` ::: ::: ::: --- ::: {.quest-step} ::: {.quest-step__meta} Quest 11 · ~10 min · Level 3 — Mapper ::: ### `map()` — one output per list element {.unnumbered} +50 XP ::: {.quest-learn} ::: {.quest-learn__row} **Packages** **`dplyr`**, **`purrr`** — `install.packages("purrr")`. ::: ::: {.quest-learn__row} **By the end you will** Split data by site into a list, **`map()`** a mean over each tibble, and read **`~ mean(.x$count)`** (purrr formula shorthand for anonymous functions). ::: ::: {.quest-learn__row} **Apply it later** Lists of spatial objects, nested model fits, or per-year data frames — **`map()`** is the idiomatic replacement for **`lapply()`** when you want consistent naming and typed variants ([Henry & Wickham, 2020](https://purrr.tidyverse.org/)). ::: ::: **What you'll learn:** **`group_split()`** returns a list of data frames. **`group_keys()`** + **`pull(site)`** gives site names for the list. **`map(list, ~ ...)`** applies the expression to each element; **`.x`** is the current element. **`~`** is shorthand for **`function(.x) ...`**. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} site_lists <- reef_transects |> group_by(site) |> # prepare grouping group_split() # list of three data frames names(site_lists) <- reef_transects |> group_by(site) |> group_keys() |> # one row per site pull(site) # character vector of site names means_list <- map(site_lists, ~ mean(.x$count)) # mean count per site's tibble means_list ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` $East [1] 10.95 $North [1] 8.8 $South [1] 9.8 ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.quest-step} ::: {.quest-step__meta} Quest 12 · ~10 min ::: ### Typed maps: `map_dbl()`, `map_chr()` {.unnumbered} +50 XP ::: {.quest-learn} ::: {.quest-learn__row} **Packages** **`purrr`**. ::: ::: {.quest-learn__row} **By the end you will** Return a **double vector** and a **character vector** directly — no **`sapply()`** simplify surprises. ::: ::: {.quest-learn__row} **Apply it later** When binding model AICs or tag IDs, insist AI use **`map_dbl()`** / **`map_chr()`** so **`c()`** and **`bind_rows()`** get predictable types. ::: ::: **What you'll learn:** **`map_dbl()`** always returns a numeric vector of length **`length(list)`**. **`map_chr()`** returns characters. **`paste(..., collapse = ", ")`** joins strings. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} map_dbl(site_lists, ~ mean(.x$count)) # numeric vector of means ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` East North South 10.95 8.80 9.80 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} map_chr(site_lists, ~ paste(unique(.x$transect_id), collapse = ", ")) # transect IDs as one string per site ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` East North South "T03, T06, T09, T12" "T01, T04, T07, T10" "T02, T05, T08, T11" ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.quest-step} ::: {.quest-step__meta} Quest 13 · ~10 min ::: ### `map2()` — two parallel vectors {.unnumbered} +50 XP ::: {.quest-learn} ::: {.quest-learn__row} **Packages** **`dplyr`**, **`purrr`**. ::: ::: {.quest-learn__row} **By the end you will** Multiply each species' total count by a species-specific mean weight using **`map2_dbl()`**, with **`.x`** and **`.y`** as the paired elements. ::: ::: {.quest-learn__row} **Apply it later** Length–weight regressions applied per species, matching survey IDs to metadata rows, or pairing lat with lon lists — if AI uses **`map()`** where two vectors align, ask for **`map2()`**. ::: ::: **What you'll learn:** **`map2_dbl(.x, .y, ~ ...)`** walks two vectors in parallel. Inside **`~`**, **`.x`** is the species name, **`.y`** is the total count. **`mean_weight_kg[[.x]]`** looks up weight by name. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} species_tbl <- reef_transects |> group_by(species) |> summarise(total = sum(count), mean_depth = mean(depth_m), .groups = "drop") # one row per species mean_weight_kg <- c( # named vector: kg per individual by species Parrotfish = 0.35, Surgeonfish = 0.55, Grouper = 2.1, Snapper = 0.8, Damselfish = 0.05 ) species_tbl |> mutate( biomass_kg = map2_dbl( # parallel walk over two columns species, total, # .x = species name, .y = total count ~ .y * mean_weight_kg[[.x]] # count × species-specific weight ) ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 5 × 4 species total mean_depth biomass_kg 1 Damselfish 272 7.59 13.6 2 Grouper 45 7.59 94.5 3 Parrotfish 88 7.59 30.8 4 Snapper 59 7.59 47.2 5 Surgeonfish 127 7.59 69.9 ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.quest-step} ::: {.quest-step__meta} Quest 14 · ~10 min ::: ### `pmap()` — one row at a time {.unnumbered} +50 XP ::: {.quest-learn} ::: {.quest-learn__row} **Packages** **`dplyr`**, **`purrr`**. ::: ::: {.quest-learn__row} **By the end you will** Classify each transect's depth as shallow / mid / deep with **`pmap_chr()`**, the row-wise mapper. ::: ::: {.quest-learn__row} **Apply it later** Complex **`ifelse()`** ladders on many columns — **`pmap()`** is clearer and easier for you (and AI) to test row by row. ::: ::: **What you'll learn:** **`distinct()`** keeps unique rows. **`pmap_chr(list(col), ~ ...)`** passes each row's values as **`..1`**, **`..2`**, …; with one column, **`..1`** or **`.x`** in a one-argument function is the depth value. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} reef_transects |> distinct(transect_id, site, depth_m) |> # one row per transect mutate( depth_class = pmap_chr( # character result, one label per row list(depth_m), # columns to pass row-wise (here, just depth) ~ if (.x < 6) "shallow" else if (.x < 11) "mid" else "deep" # .x is depth for this row ) ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 12 × 4 transect_id site depth_m depth_class 1 T01 North 6.2 mid 2 T02 South 5.2 shallow 3 T03 East 6.3 mid 4 T04 North 8.5 mid 5 T05 South 10.4 mid 6 T06 East 9.9 mid 7 T07 North 7.7 mid 8 T08 South 9.6 mid 9 T09 East 4.1 shallow 10 T10 North 7.9 mid 11 T11 South 11.9 deep 12 T12 East 3.4 shallow ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.quest-step} ::: {.quest-step__meta} Quest 15 · ~10 min ::: ### `map_dfr()` — stack many data frames {.unnumbered} +50 XP ::: {.quest-learn} ::: {.quest-learn__row} **Packages** **`purrr`**, **`ggplot2`**, **`tibble`**. ::: ::: {.quest-learn__row} **By the end you will** Simulate eight transects by mapping **`simulate_transect()`** over IDs and row-binding with **`map_dfr()`**. ::: ::: {.quest-learn__row} **Apply it later** Monte Carlo abundance, bootstrap transects, or “what if we sampled again?” — **`map_dfr()`** is the standard simulation stacker. ::: ::: **What you'll learn:** **`rpois(n, lambda)`** draws Poisson counts. **`simulate_transect()`** returns a small tibble. **`map_dfr(ids, fun, ...)`** calls **`fun`** for each ID and **`bind_rows()`** the results. **`sprintf()`** formats ID strings. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} simulate_transect <- function(transect_id, lambda = 8) { # one simulated transect tibble::tibble( transect_id = transect_id, # label this draw count = rpois(5, lambda = lambda) # five species counts at this lambda ) } sim_ids <- sprintf("sim_%02d", 1:8) # eight simulation IDs sim_data <- map_dfr(sim_ids, simulate_transect, lambda = 10) # stack eight tibbles ggplot(sim_data, aes(x = transect_id, y = count)) + # plot simulated draws geom_point(size = 2) + theme_minimal() + labs(title = "Eight simulated transects via map_dfr()") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-html/quest-15-1.png){width=840} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.quest-step} ::: {.quest-step__meta} Quest 16 · ~10 min ::: ### `safely()` — errors become data {.unnumbered} +50 XP ::: {.quest-learn} ::: {.quest-learn__row} **Packages** **`purrr`**. ::: ::: {.quest-learn__row} **By the end you will** Wrap a fragile ratio in **`safely()`** so failed divisions return **`error`** instead of stopping your script — then inspect with **`map()`**. ::: ::: {.quest-learn__row} **Apply it later** Batch-reading receiver folders, fitting GLMMs per tag, parsing messy CSVs — **`safely()`** + **`map()`** is how you QC 200 files without **`tryCatch()`** soup. Tell AI: *“wrap the reader in `safely()`.”* ::: ::: **What you'll learn:** **`safely(f)`** returns a new function that always gives a list with **`result`** and **`error`**. **`map_lgl()`** maps to logical. **`is.null(.x$error)`** means success. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} safe_ratio <- safely(function(num, den) { # safely wraps our division num / den # fails when den is 0 }) tests <- list( # three test cases safe_ratio(10, 2), # ok safe_ratio(3, 0), # error captured safe_ratio(NA, 5) # may return NA in result ) map_lgl(tests, ~ is.null(.x$error)) # TRUE where no error ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] TRUE TRUE TRUE ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} map(tests, ~ .x$result) # extract results (NULL where error) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [[1]] [1] 5 [[2]] [1] Inf [[3]] [1] NA ``` ::: ::: ::: --- ::: {.quest-step} ::: {.quest-step__meta} Quest 17 · ~10 min · Level 4 — Parallel diver ::: ### `furrr` — parallel `map()` when tasks are independent {.unnumbered} +50 XP ::: {.quest-learn} ::: {.quest-learn__row} **Packages** **`dplyr`**, **`purrr`**, **`future`**, **`furrr`** — `install.packages(c("future", "furrr"))`. ::: ::: {.quest-learn__row} **By the end you will** Fit a Poisson **`glm()`** per species on separate workers with **`future_map()`**, after **`plan(multisession)`**. ::: ::: {.quest-learn__row} **Apply it later** Bootstrap CIs, many MCMC restarts, or per-AnimalID home-range models — parallelise only when each task is **independent** ([Bengtsson, 2021](https://doi.org/10.32614/RJ-2021-048)). Ask AI for **`furrr`**, not random **`parallel::mclapply`** on Windows. ::: ::: **What you'll learn:** **`plan(multisession, workers = 2)`** starts a small worker pool. **`future_map()`** like **`map()`** but parallel. **`tryCatch()`** catches errors inside **`fit_poisson`**. **`glm(..., family = poisson)`** fits count vs depth. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} if (requireNamespace("furrr", quietly = TRUE)) { # only run if furrr is installed library(furrr) # parallel purrr plan(multisession, workers = 2) # two background R sessions species_splits <- reef_transects |> group_split(species) # one tibble per species fit_poisson <- function(df) { # one glm per species table tryCatch( glm(count ~ depth_m, data = df, family = poisson), # Poisson regression error = function(e) NULL # return NULL instead of stopping ) } models <- future_map(species_splits, fit_poisson) # fit models in parallel map_lgl(models, ~ !is.null(.x)) # which species fit successfully? } else { message("Install furrr to run Quest 17 locally: install.packages('furrr')") } ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] TRUE TRUE TRUE TRUE TRUE ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.quest-step} ::: {.quest-step__meta} Quest 18 · ~10 min ::: ### `walk()` — map for side effects only {.unnumbered} +50 XP ::: {.quest-learn} ::: {.quest-learn__row} **Packages** **`dplyr`**, **`purrr`**. ::: ::: {.quest-learn__row} **By the end you will** Print a one-line site report for each list element with **`walk()`**, which discards return values (unlike **`map()`**). ::: ::: {.quest-learn__row} **Apply it later** **`ggsave()`** per tag, **`write_csv()`** per trip, **`message()`** QC logs — **`walk2()`** pairs IDs with plots. AI loves **`map()`** for this; **`walk()`** is the correct tool. ::: ::: **What you'll learn:** **`walk(list, ~ ...)`** runs code for each element and returns **`list`** invisibly. **`message()`** prints to the console without stopping. **`paste0()`** builds strings. **`n_distinct()`** counts unique values. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} report_site <- function(df) { # one-line summary string paste0( unique(df$site)[1], # site name ": ", n_distinct(df$transect_id), # how many transects " transects, total count = ", sum(df$count) # sum of counts ) } walk(site_lists, ~ message(report_site(.x))) # message each report; return value ignored invisible(NULL) # chunk returns quietly in HTML ``` ::: ::: ::: {.quest-step} ::: {.quest-step__meta} Quest 19 · ~10 min ::: ### data.table: `[i, j, by]` {.unnumbered} +50 XP ::: {.quest-learn} ::: {.quest-learn__row} **Packages** **`data.table`** — `install.packages("data.table")`. ::: ::: {.quest-learn__row} **By the end you will** Compute mean count and **`.N`** (row count) per **`site`** in one **`DT[i, j, by]`** expression. ::: ::: {.quest-learn__row} **Apply it later** Million-row acoustic detection tables — data.table is what you want when AI's dplyr pipeline is correct but slow. Learn to **read** `[ , .(...), by = ...]` even if you write dplyr day-to-day ([Dowle & Srinivasan, 2024](https://doi.org/10.32614/RJ-2024-001)). ::: ::: **What you'll learn:** **`as.data.table()`** converts without copying when possible. Syntax **`DT[i, j, by]`**: **`i`** filters rows (empty = all), **`j`** computes columns, **`by`** groups. **`.(...)`** lists output columns. **`.N`** is group size. **`order(-mean_count)`** sorts descending. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} dt <- as.data.table(reef_transects) # data.table version of our reprex dt[ , # i: no row filter — use all rows .( # j: columns to return mean_count = mean(count), # average count in group n = .N # number of rows in group ), by = site # group by site ][order(-mean_count)] # sort result by mean_count descending ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` site mean_count n 1: East 10.95 20 2: South 9.80 20 3: North 8.80 20 ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.quest-step} ::: {.quest-step__meta} Quest 20 · ~10 min ::: ### `.SD` — group subsets in data.table {.unnumbered} +50 XP ::: {.quest-learn} ::: {.quest-learn__row} **Packages** **`data.table`**, **`ggplot2`**, **`plotly`**, **`tibble`** (for **`as_tibble()`** before ggplot). ::: ::: {.quest-learn__row} **By the end you will** Summarise **`mean(count)`** and **`mean(depth_m)`** by **`site`** and **`species`**, then plot grouped bars — same mental model as **`group_by()` + `summarise()`**. ::: ::: {.quest-learn__row} **Apply it later** When AI pastes data.table code into your telemetry pipeline, recognise **`.(site, species)`** as grouping and **`.SD`** as “columns available in this group” — then decide if you keep it or translate to dplyr you can maintain. ::: ::: **What you'll learn:** **`by = .(site, species)`** groups on two columns. **`mean(count)`** in **`j`** is evaluated within each group. **`as_tibble()`** converts for **`ggplot()`**. **`geom_col(position = "dodge")`** places bars side by side. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} dt[ , .( mean_count = mean(count), # average abundance mean_depth = mean(depth_m) # average depth ), by = .(site, species) # cross of site and species ] ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` site species mean_count mean_depth 1: North Parrotfish 5.25 7.575 2: North Surgeonfish 10.25 7.575 3: North Grouper 2.00 7.575 4: North Snapper 4.50 7.575 5: North Damselfish 22.00 7.575 6: South Parrotfish 8.00 9.275 7: South Surgeonfish 10.00 9.275 8: South Grouper 3.25 9.275 9: South Snapper 5.75 9.275 10: South Damselfish 22.00 9.275 11: East Parrotfish 8.75 5.925 12: East Surgeonfish 11.50 5.925 13: East Grouper 6.00 5.925 14: East Snapper 4.50 5.925 15: East Damselfish 24.00 5.925 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} plot_20 <- dt[ , .(mean_count = mean(count)), # simpler summary for plotting by = .(site, species) ] |> as_tibble() |> # ggplot prefers tibble ggplot(aes(x = species, y = mean_count, fill = site)) + # dodged bars by site geom_col(position = "dodge") + theme_minimal(base_size = 12) + theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 35, hjust = 1)) + # readable species names labs(title = "Group means — data.table → ggplot") plotly::ggplotly(plot_20) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ::: --- ## Season 1 complete — you beat the paste buffer Twenty quests ≈ **1,000 XP**. You can now **read** functional ecology code, **critique** AI output, and **request** the right tool (`map2`, `safely`, `walk`, `[ , , by]`). ### Roadmap · quests 21–100 | Season | Quests | Focus | ~time | |--------|--------|--------|-------| | 2 | 21–30 | `map_dfc`, list-columns, **`unnest()`** | 1 week | | 3 | 31–40 | **`reduce()`**, spatial list columns | 1 week | | 4 | 41–50 | **`furrr`** bootstrap CIs per reef | 1 week | | 5 | 51–60 | data.table joins on tag metadata | 1 week | | 6 | 61–70 | **`possibly()`**, **`quietly()`**, failed models | 1 week | | 7 | 71–80 | purrr + **sf** per-animal ranges | 2 weeks | | 8 | 81–90 | temporal **`slide()`** patterns | 2 weeks | | 9 | 91–95 | your first package function | 1 week | | 10 | 96–100 | capstone: refactor telemetry functionally | 1 week | ::: {.callout-important appearance="simple"} ## Reprex for humans and AI When you ask for help — from a forum or from a model — paste **data + sessionInfo()**, not a screenshot: ``` r set.seed(427) # ... reef_transects chunk from above ... sessionInfo() ``` Prompt tip: *“Use my `summarise_abundance()` function; do not introduce new column names.”* ::: ## References - Bengtsson, H.
